title: Tour de CLARIN: Interview with Filip Zeman
content:
This conversation was led by Laura Gusan

Filip Zeman is a PhD researcher at the Autonomous University of Madrid. He has a bachelor's degree in English and Modern Languages from the University of Exeter, UK, and a master's degree in Applied Linguistics for language teaching from the Autonomous University of Madrid. His doctoral project focuses on a new method of teaching Spanish as a foreign language.
snippet: This conversation was led by Laura Gusan
creator: Laura Gusan
sourceDescription: Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure

title: Tour de CLARIN: Interview with Guna R'abante-Buša
content:
Guna Rābante-Buša was selected to represent the CLARIN-LV consortium because her research and other activities closely align with CLARIN's goals. As a linguistics PhD student specialising in phonetics, she has not only conducted extensive research using Latvian speech corpora but has also actively contributed to the development of several recent speech datasets, which are registered in the CLARIN-LV repository. Guna also actively participates in teaching activities and the development of new course materials, where she actively promotes CLARIN-LV speech resources. Finally, she has also provided valuable support in organising several CLARIN-LV events, including the recent CLARIN and DARIAH Spring conference.
 

Inguna Skadiņa, CLARIN National Coordinator for Latvia

This conversation was led by Laura Gusan

title: New GA President and Vice-president
content:
As of 1st of May the CLARIN

Marían Rodriguez Parilla is Head of the area in charge of the management of ERDF funds and Recovery and Resilience funds at the Deputy General Direction of Consortia, Organizations, and International Scientific Infrastructure at the Ministry of Science, Education and Universities. Additionally, she is responsible for the Social Science and Humanities International Scientific Infrastructures being the Spanish representative at the General Assembly of CLARIN, DARIAH and ESS ERIC.

Lea Bühlmann is Scientific Advisor at the Swiss State Secretariat for Education, Research and Innovation (SERI), where she is responsible for Switzerland’s participation in European Research Infrastructure Consortia (ERICs) in the Social Sciences and Humanities. She contributes to the development of Switzerland’s ERIC policy and represents Switzerland in several SSH ERICs, including CLARIN ERIC.

title: Consortium Highlights: CLARIN.SI
content: In 2026, the CLARIN.SI repository has, so far, accessioned 100 new items, the largest share of which are samples of e-books and audiobooks made available through a project of the Slovenian Book Agency. Of the remaining items, the largest, and likely the most interesting, is the South Slavic web corpus collection CLASSLA-web 2.0, a large-scale, comparable set of web corpora covering all seven South Slavic languages: Slovenian, Croatian, Bosnian, Montenegrin, Serbian, Macedonian, and Bulgarian. The collection was produced within the scope of the CLASSLA K-centre, in which CLARIN.SI has a leading role, and contains over 17 billion words and 28 million deduplicated texts. Each text is annotated with rich metadata, such as predicted genre and topic categories, while the texts themselves are linguistically annotated with Universal Dependencies PoS tags, morphological features, and lemmas. Another interesting corpus is the 'Disasters corpus in classical Arabic sources' (DiCCAS), submitted by authors from the University of Bologna, and the first Arabic corpus deposited in our repository. It contains about 300 thousand words from 10 classical Arabic texts, with annotated disaster-related spans and linguistically annotated tokens. Both CLASSLA-web 2.0 and DiCCAS are also available for online analysis in CLARIN.SI concordancers.
snippet: In 2026, the CLARIN.SI repository has, so far, accessioned 100 new items, the largest share of which are samples of e-books and audiobooks made available through a project of the Slovenian Book
creator: Laura Gusan
sourceDescription: Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure

title: Tour de CLARIN: Interview with Mojca Brglez
content:
The PhD by Mojca Brglez addresses the identification and interpretation of metaphors in Slovenian using computational methods, and is one of the first studies of this kind for Slovenian. It develops, applies, and evaluates several diverse and complex methods for this purpose, including word embeddings for predicting metaphoricity, and uses them to detect changes in the meanings of Slovenian words over time. As part of her PhD, Mojca also created a metaphor-oriented dataset and published it in the CLARIN.SI repository. She is also the co-author of five other Slovenian language resources deposited in the repository, covering topics ranging from genre identification and word associations to semantics and emotions.

Tomaž Erjavec, CLARIN National Coordinator for Slovenia

This conversation was led by Laura Gusan

title: Tour de CLARIN: Interview with Kajsa Gullberg
content:
Kajsa Gullberg's PhD project investigates the real-time processing differences between true and invented narratives. She has conducted two extensive data collections, gathering stories in both spoken and written formats. Her methodology includes audio recordings, keystroke logging with Scriptlog, mobile eye tracking, and video recordings, conducted both remotely via Zoom and in a studio setting. The research is highly relevant to the CLARIN community as it enhances our understanding of language processing, provides valuable insights for forensic applications, and integrates multiple data sources. It will also result in a comprehensive corpus to be released in 2026, containing over 200.000 words and nearly 700 files. Participation in the CLARIN conference has provided Kajsa with an excellent opportunity to network, finalise her work, and receive critical feedback from the community's diverse expertise. 

Sara Stymne, CLARIN National Coordinator for Sweden

The conversation was led by Laura Gusan

title: Zeit.shift MOOC - Digital in Yesterday's Future
content:
What do newspapers tell us about our lives 100 years ago? How are newspapers digitised and made accessible via web portals? How can I research historical newspapers available online in order to formulate a scientific question? How do the digital humanities use computer-based methods to index historical text documents?

In the Zeit.shift-MOOC - Digital in Yesterday's Future you will work with historical daily newspapers from North, East and South Tyrol. We will introduce you to newspapers as online sources for research in the context of scientific questions. We will prepare selected topics from the newspapers as examples to give you an understanding of the range of historical texts and to make it easier for you to find an exciting topic for a scientific paper.

This MOOC is primarily aimed at pupils and anyone who wants to work actively with newspapers available online. We will guide you directly into the historical source and support you in interrogating the text sources and in using them in a contemporary way. We will illustrate the potential of digital humanities and give an insight into some computational linguistic techniques that facilitate work with historical newspapers.

creator: Iulianna van der Lek-Ciudin
sourceDescription: Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure

title: Data Stewardship
content:
UiT The Arctic University of Norway contributed to the development of a course in Data Stewardship within the framework of DocEnhance project (2020-2022). The course consists of three modules, all openly available with CC BY 4.0 license in the DocEnhance Moodle LMS: https://courses.docenhance.eu/. 

Anyone is free to do the Module 1 course without creating an account and logging in. To do the full course with all three Modules, and to earn credits, the courses need to be organized by an HE institution, and participants need to be registered and enrolled. 

  • Module 1 is a MOOC that goes through theory and best practice within the many steps and aspects of data stewardship. The module is composed of an introduction, nine thematic sections and a multiple-choice exam. The MOOC contains video lectures, text, and self-paced activities, and includes required and recommended readings. Having passed the Module 1 exam is a requirement to participate in Modules 2 and 3.
